did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used an Augmented Intelligence Dermal Imager (AID) which employs a deep learning-based augmented intelligence algorithm to analyze hypodermoscopy images of atypical pigmented lesions. The AI system performed deep tissue sequencing to extract features and calculate malignancy risk factors for melanoma diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'Original Article' and 'research-article'. It presents original experimental data from a pilot study involving 8 patients and 9 atypical pigmented lesions evaluated using an Augmented Intelligence Dermal Imager (AID). The study includes specific Methods, Results (sensitivity 82%, specificity 61%), and Case descriptions, indicating it produces new empirical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Deep Tissue Sequencing Using Hypodermoscopy and Augmented Intelligence to Analyze Atypical Pigmented Lesions
journal: Journal of Cutaneous Medicine and Surgery
brief_summary: This study evaluated the performance of an Augmented Intelligence Dermal Imager (AID), which combines hypodermoscopy with a deep learning-based augmented intelligence algorithm, for analyzing atypical pigmented lesions. The system was tested on nine lesions from eight patients prior to surgical removal, with results compared against final histopathological diagnoses. The AID system demonstrated a sensitivity of 82% and a specificity of 61% in its internal risk assessment algorithms. The AI model was trained on over 13,000 lesions from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset. The authors concluded that the system's malignancy risk factor calculation could help justify biopsies, though further validation is needed to establish clinical utility.

lesion_types_studied.details: Final histopathological diagnosis for cases 1 (both lesions), 5, 7, and 8. Also identified via prior biopsy in cases 1 and 7.
Final histopathological diagnosis for cases 2, 4, 6, and 7 (focal). Identified via prior biopsy in cases 2, 4, and 6.
Final histopathological diagnosis for case 3. Used as a benign comparator lesion in the study.

The general category of lesions evaluated by the AID system prior to surgical removal. Includes the specific malignant and benign cases listed above. Clinically diagnosed as 'severely atypical' in case 3.

all_task_types.target: Malignancy risk assessment (benign vs. malignant/diagnostic category)
all_task_types.details: The Augmented Intelligence Dermal Imager (AID) system performs a binary classification task to assess atypical pigmented lesions as malignant or benign. The system calculates a 'malignancy risk factor' (a probability score used for classification) and reports sensitivity (82%) and specificity (61%) based on its internal risk assessment algorithms. The output is used to justify biopsy, indicating a diagnostic classification task rather than a continuous regression of physical measurements.

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public database of dermoscopy images collected from leading clinical centers internationally and acquired from a variety of devices. The images were captured from patients with different ages (infants to elderly, mostly aged 10 to 20 years) and sexes (40% female, 60% male). The dataset contains over 13,000 known lesions with definitive prior diagnoses.

use.training: Used to train the deep tissue sequencer (augmented intelligence algorithm) by providing verified dermoscopy images to learn features and characteristics of lesions.
use.other: Served as the base database for the AI model's learned characteristics.

datasets_used.name: Clinical Pilot Cohort (AID Study Dataset)
datasets_used.description: A private, internal clinical dataset consisting of hypodermoscopy and dermoscopy images of atypical pigmented lesions evaluated on 8 patients prior to surgical removal. The lesions were assessed using the Augmented Intelligence Dermal Imager (AID) and compared against final histopathological diagnoses.

use.testing: Used to evaluate the performance of the AID system's internal risk assessment algorithms, calculating sensitivity (82%) and specificity (61%).
use.other: Used for pilot study validation in a clinical setting to assess the system's ability to calculate malignancy risk factors.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported descriptive performance metrics (sensitivity of 82% and specificity of 61%) for the Augmented Intelligence Dermal Imager (AID) system on a small pilot cohort of 9 lesions, but did not perform statistical comparisons or hypothesis tests.

ISIC Dataset (Training/Pre-training).metric: Sensitivity and Specificity on a set of 8206 cases
ISIC Dataset (Training/Pre-training).notes: The deep tissue sequencer was initially trained on a database of 13,000 dermoscopy images (approximately 10% malignant) from the ISIC dataset. It achieved 91% sensitivity and 75% specificity on a separate set of 8,206 cases before being adapted for hypodermoscopy via transfer learning.

Clinical Pilot Cohort (AID Study Dataset.metric: Sensitivity and Specificity based on internal risk assessment algorithms
Clinical Pilot Cohort (AID Study Dataset.notes: The AID system was evaluated on 9 atypical pigmented lesions from 8 patients. The malignancy risk factors for each case are detailed in Table 1. Case 1 had two lesions (both MIS) with risk factors of 0.99. Case 2 (IMM) had a risk factor of 0.99. Case 3 (Seborrheic keratosis) had a risk factor of 0.75. Case 4 (IMM) had a risk factor of 1.0. Case 5 (MIS) had a risk factor of 0.99. Case 6 (IMM) had a risk factor of 0.93. Case 7 (MIS with focal IMM) had a risk factor of 0.69. Case 8 (Unequivocal MIS) had a risk factor of 0.99.

architecture_used.main_model: Deep Tissue Sequencer (part of the Augmented Intelligence Dermal Imager system)
architecture_used.pretraining: Trained on a database of over 13,000 known dermoscopy images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset (approximately 10% malignant cases). The model was initially discovered/validated on a set of 8,206 cases achieving 91% sensitivity and 75% specificity, then adapted for hypodermoscopy via transfer learning.

architecture_details.mechanism: Splits hypodermoscopy imaging data into parallel streams of increasingly more abstract skin tissue traits learned from the database, which are then merged into a single deep tissue sequence. This sequence encodes underlying characteristics of the lesion to allow comparison with a database of generated sequences from past cases.
architecture_details.output: Produces a malignancy risk factor and false-colored diffusion maps representing melanin concentration and subsurface/deep tissue structure (0.1-0.8 mm and 0.8-1.5 mm depths).

training_details.method: Transfer learning was used to adapt the sequencer from dermoscopy to hypodermoscopy features.

classification_or_output_head.description: Calculates a malignancy risk factor (probability score) for the lesion compared to known melanoma features based on the extracted deep tissue sequences.

conclusions: The Augmented Intelligence Dermal Imager (AID) system, which combines dermoscopy, hypodermoscopy, and a trained augmented intelligence algorithm, can produce diffusion maps representing lesion features compared to learned characteristics from a database of known dermoscopy images.
The AID system demonstrated a sensitivity of 82% and a specificity of 61% in its internal risk assessment algorithms when evaluating atypical pigmented lesions prior to surgical removal.
The malignancy risk factor calculated by the AID system has the potential to provide information to justify the biopsy of atypical pigmented lesions.

The current AID system exhibits analysis biases present in the training ISIC dataset, which consists largely of melanoma cases in patients aged 10 to 20 years and lacks common characteristics of older skin types.
The study represents an early assessment of the AID in a clinical setting using hypodermoscopy, whereas prior assessments were conducted on digital images.
Further validation and analysis are required to extrapolate the clinical usefulness of the information received from these evaluations.
More comprehensive studies are needed to validate the proposed approach and evaluate the efficacy of using the AID system for clinical diagnosis of atypical pigmented lesions.
important_remarks: The study is a pilot evaluation with a very small sample size (9 lesions from 8 patients), limiting statistical power and generalizability.

The AI model was trained on the ISIC dataset, which exhibits population bias: the majority of training cases are melanomas in patients aged 10 to 20 years, lacking characteristics of older skin types common in clinical practice.
The system demonstrated low specificity (61%) in this clinical setting, indicating a high potential for false positives and unnecessary biopsies.

The study lacks external validation; it was conducted at a single nonhospital surgical facility, and the authors explicitly state that further comprehensive studies are needed to extrapolate clinical usefulness.
There is a potential conflict of interest as the authors are employees and inventors of the Augmented Intelligence Dermal Imager (AID) system.

The study represents an early assessment of the AID in a clinical setting using hypodermoscopy, whereas prior validations were conducted on digital dermoscopy images, introducing modality differences.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing or confidence intervals were reported for the performance metrics.
The system's risk factors are based on limited training and testing databases available at the time, and accuracy is expected to improve as the database expands.
